volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 4.8.883b - Section 4.9.993c)

sections.label: Section 4.8.883b
sections.translation: They shall be given , say : do answer him .

sections.label: Section 4.8.883c
sections.translation: I ’ ll give them .

sections.label: Section 4.8.884
sections.original: Quid nunc , impure ? numquid debetur tibi ?
sections.translation: Well , now , filthy fellow is there anything owing to you ?

sections.label: Section 4.8.885
sections.original: quid illí ? quid illum morte territas ?
sections.translation: Why are you plaguing him ? Why are you frightening him about death ? Both I and he wish you every ill luck . If you have a sword , still we have a spit at home ; with which , in fact , if you provoke me ,

sections.label: Section 4.8.890
sections.original: iam dudum hercle equidem sentio , suspicio
sections.translation: I ’ ll make you more full of holes than a rat ’ s skin [*] when caught in a trap . I ’ faith , for my part , some time since I found out what suspicion it was that tormented you ; namely , that he was with that wife of yours .

sections.label: Section 4.8.892
sections.translation: Yes ; and so he is .

sections.label: Section 4.8.892b
sections.translation: So may Jupiter , Juno , Ceres , Minerva , Hope , Latona , Ops , Virtue , Venus , Castor , Pollux , Mars , Mercury , Hercules ,

sections.label: Section 4.8.895
sections.original: Summanus Sol Saturnus dique omnes ament ,
sections.translation: Summanus [*] , the Sun , Saturn , and all the Deities , prosper me , he neither reclines with her , nor walks , nor kisses , nor does that which is wont to be reported .

sections.label: Section 4.8.898
sections.original: Vt iurat ! servat me ille suis periuriis .
sections.translation: How he does swear ! He ’ s saving me , however , by his perjury .

sections.label: Section 4.8.899
sections.translation: Where then , at this moment , is Mnesilochus ?

sections.label: Section 4.8.899b
sections.translation: His father has sent him into the country .

sections.label: Section 4.8.900
sections.original: illa autem in arcem ábiit aedem visere
sections.translation: And she has gone hence to the citadel , to see the temple of Minerva . The door ’ s open now ; go in , and see if he ’ s there .

sections.label: Section 4.8.902
sections.translation: I ’ ll be off to the Forum next .

sections.label: Section 4.8.902b
sections.translation: Or rather , by my troth , to very perdition .

sections.label: Section 4.8.903
sections.translation: Am I to demand this gold to-day ?

sections.label: Section 4.8.903b
sections.translation: Demand it , and go hang yourself ; don ’ t you suppose , you worthless fellow , that we shall be entreating of you . ( Exit CLEOMACHUS .)

sections.label: Section 4.8.905
sections.original: ille est amotus . sine me ( per te , ere , opsecro
sections.translation: He ’ s taken himself off . Permit me , master , I entreat you by the immortal Gods , to go in-doors here to your son .

sections.label: Section 4.8.907
sections.translation: But why go in there ?

sections.label: Section 4.8.907b
sections.translation: That with many words I may rebuke him , since after this fashion he has been going on this way .

sections.label: Section 4.8.909
sections.original: Immo oro ut facias , Chrysale , et ted opsecro ,
sections.translation: Well , I beg you will do so , Chrysalus ; and I entreat you not to spare him in your talking to him .

sections.label: Section 4.8.910b
sections.translation: And do you instruct even myself ? Isn ’ t it sufficient , if this day he shall hear from me more harsh things than ever Clinias heard [*] from Demetrius ?

sections.label: Section 4.8.913
sections.original: Lippi illic oculi servos est simillimus :
sections.translation: This servant of mine is very like a running eye ; if you have it not , you don ’ t wish for it or desire it ;

sections.label: Section 4.8.915
sections.original: si est , abstinere quin attingas non queas .
sections.translation: if you have it , you can ’ t keep off from rubbing it . But if he hadn ’ t , by lucky chance , been here to-day , the Captain would have surprised Mnesilochus with his wife , and have killed him as an adulterer detected in the fact . Now , in a manner have I ransomed my son with the two hundred Philippeans

sections.label: Section 4.8.920
sections.original: quos dare promisi militi : quos non dabo
sections.translation: which I have promised to give to the Captain ; which , however , I shall not rashly pay him down , before I have met with my son . By my troth , I ’ ll never rashly give credence in anything to Chrysalus . But I have a mind even once again to read over this letter ; ’ tis right that when a letter is sealed we should give credence to it .

sections.label: Section 4.9.925
sections.original: Atrídae duo fratrés cluent fecísse facinus máxumum ,
sections.translation: The two brothers , the sons of Atreus , are said to have done a most famous deed , when , with arms , and horses , and an army , and with chosen warriors , and with ships a thousand in number , after the tenth year , they subdued Pergamus , the native land of Priam , founded by hands divine . Not more decidedly did it fall by the engine of war , than I shall storm my master here ,

sections.label: Section 4.9.930
sections.original: sine classe sineque exercitu et tanto numero militum .
sections.translation: without a fleet , and without an army and so great array of soldiers . I have won , I have taken by storm this gold from his father for my master ’ s son , in his amour . Now , before the old man comes here , I wish to lament until he does come out . O Troy ! O my country ! O Pergamus ! O Priam ! old man , you are undone , you , who ’ ll be wretchedly and shockingly choused out of four hundred golden Philippeans .

sections.label: Section 4.9.935
sections.original: nam ego has tabellas obsignatas consignatas quas fero
sections.translation: For those tablets , sealed on the one side and on the other , they are not tablets , but the horse which the Greeks sent , of wood . Pistoclerus is the Epeus [*] ; from him were these received . Mnesilochus is the Simon left behind . Behold him ! not in Achilles ’ tomb , but on a couch he reclines : he has Bacchis with him ; just as the other formerly had the fire with which to give the signal ;

sections.label: Section 4.9.940
sections.original: hunc ipsum exurit ; ego sum Vlixes , cuius consilio haec gerunt .
sections.translation: so now does she inflame himself . I am Ulysses , by whose advice they do these things . Then , the characters which there are written , are the soldiers in this horse , armed and of high courage . So even thus far has the matter prospered with me . This horse , too , will be making his attack , not on a citadel , but on a coffer [*] A ruin , a destruction , a cleaner-out of the old man ’ s gold , will this horse prove this day .

sections.label: Section 4.9.945
sections.original: nostro seni huic stolido , ei profecto nomen facio ego Ilio ;
sections.translation: To this silly old man of ours , in fact , I give the name of Ilium ; the Captain is Menelaüs ; I , Agamemnon ; I , too , am Ulysses , the son of Laërtes ; Mnesilochus is Alexander [*] , who will be the destruction of his father ’ s fortunes ; he has borne off the Helen , on whose account I am now carrying on the siege of Ilium . For there I have heard say that Ulysses was both bold and full of mischief , just as I am .

sections.label: Section 4.9.950
sections.original: ín dolis ego prénsus sum , ille mendícans paene invéntus interiit ,
sections.translation: I have been detected in my tricks — he , discovered in a beggar ’ s guise , had almost perished , while he was spying out there the doings of the Trojans . Similarly has it happened to myself to-day . I have been bound , but by my devices I have redeemed myself ; he , too , preserved himself by artifice . I have heard that there were three destinies [*] attending Troy , which were fatal to it ; if the statue should be lost from the citadel ; whereas the second was the death of Troilus ;

sections.label: Section 4.9.955
sections.original: tertium , cum portae Phrygiae limen superum scinderetur :
sections.translation: the third was when the upper lintel of the Phrygian gate [*] should be demolished . Just so are there three fatalities for this Troy of ours , corresponding with those three ; for , first of all , when , a short time since , as I told our old gentleman the lying story about his host , and the gold , and the bark , then , that instant , did I steal the statue [*] from the citadel . And even then two fatalities were remaining , and no further had I taken this city .

sections.label: Section 4.9.960
sections.original: post ubi tabellas ad senem detuli , ibi occidi Troilum ,
sections.translation: Afterwards , when I carried the letter to the old man , then I killed my Troilus . When he supposed , just now , that Mnesilochus was with the Captain ’ s wife , from that , with difficulty , did I disengage myself . And that danger do I compare to what they say , how that Ulysses , recognized by Helen [*] , was betrayed to Hecuba . But as , in olden time , by his coaxing arts , he liberated himself from her , and persuaded her to let him go ,

sections.label: Section 4.9.965
sections.original: ítem ego dolis me illo éxtuli e períclo et decepí senem .
sections.translation: so I , by my devices , have rescued myself from the danger , and have deceived the old man . Afterwards , I engaged with the blustering Captain , who , unarmed , takes cities with his words , and there I repulsed my man . Then I engaged in fight with the old gentleman ; straightway by one lying device did I vanquish him ; by one blow , in a moment , did I take the spoils away from him . He now will give the two hundred Philippean pieces to the Captain ,

sections.label: Section 4.9.970
sections.translation: which he has promised that he will give . ⋯ Now , I have occasion for another two hundred , to be distributed when Ilium is taken , that there may be the usual draught of honeyed wine [*] with which the soldiers may celebrate their triumph . But this Priam is far superior to him of old . Not fifty sons only has he , but four hundred , and all choice ones , without a blemish ;

sections.label: Section 4.9.975
sections.original: eos égo hodie omnis contruncabo duóbus solis ictibus .
sections.translation: all these this day will I cut off at two single blows . Now , if there were any purchaser for this Priam of ours , I would sell the old fellow in the lump [*] , whom I have on sale the moment that J shall have taken the city . But , lo ! I see our Priam standing before the door ; I ’ ll go and speak to him .

sections.label: Section 4.9.979
sections.translation: Pray , whose voice is it that sounds near me ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.979b
sections.translation: O Nicobulus !

sections.label: Section 4.9.979c
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.980
sections.original: quíd quod te misi , écquid egísti ?
sections.translation: Well , have you done aught of what I sent you upon ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.980b
sections.translation: What — ask you that ? Step closer this way .

sections.label: Section 4.9.980c
sections.translation: Well , I do come closer .

sections.label: Section 4.9.981
sections.original: Optumus sum oratór . ad lacrumas coégi hominem cástigando
sections.translation: I am an excellent pleader . By rebuking him , and by whatever hard language I really was able to think of , I forced the fellow to tears .

sections.label: Section 4.9.982b
sections.translation: What did he say ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.982c
sections.translation: He uttered not a word : crying , he listened in silence to what I was saying ; in silence he wrote down on his tablets ; these sealed he gave to me ;

sections.label: Section 4.9.985
sections.original: tíbi me iussit dare , sed metuo , né idem cantent quod priores .
sections.translation: he bade me deliver them to you . ( Gives him the tablets .) But I am afraid that they sing to the same tune that the former ones did . Observe the seal , is it his ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.986b
sections.translation: I recognize it . I ’ d like to read them over .

sections.label: Section 4.9.986c
sections.translation: Read them through . ( Aside .) Now is the upper lintel [*] being cut down , now is the destruction of Troy near at hand . The wooden horse is shaking it right cleverly .

sections.label: Section 4.9.988b
sections.original: Chrysále , ades , dum ego has pérlego .
sections.translation: Chrysalus , just step here while I am reading these through .

sections.label: Section 4.9.988a
sections.original: Quid mé tibi adesse opus ést ?
sections.translation: Prithee , what need is there for me to be near you ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.989
sections.original: út scias quae hic scrípta sient .
sections.translation: That you may know what ’ s written here .

sections.label: Section 4.9.989a
sections.translation: I don ’ t care , and I don ’ t wish to know .

sections.label: Section 4.9.990
sections.translation: Still , do come here .

sections.label: Section 4.9.990b
sections.translation: What need is there ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.990c
sections.translation: Hold your tongue .

sections.label: Section 4.9.990ab
sections.translation: I ’ ll stay .

sections.label: Section 4.9.991
sections.translation: Bless me ! the letters are small .

sections.label: Section 4.9.991b
sections.translation: To one , indeed , who cannot see well with his eyes but they are quite large enough for one who can see well .

sections.label: Section 4.9.993
sections.translation: I won ’ t , I say .

sections.label: Section 4.9.993b
sections.translation: But I will , I say .

sections.label: Section 4.9.993c
sections.translation: What need is there ?
